coredata.dc:title: Quinoid to benzenoid transition-driven glutathione sensing: Dual-emission carbon dots and smartphone-based ratiometric fluorescence analysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Ali, Al-Montaser Bellah H.
Mahmoud, Ashraf M.
Jardan, Yousef A. Bin
Mostafa, Aya M.
Barker, James
El-Wekil, Mohamed M.
coredata.dc:description: This study presents a novel and sensitive method for the determination of glutathione (GSH), a crucial antioxidant and cellular protectant, using dual blue/orange-emitting carbon dots (BOCDs) and phenolphthalein (PHP). The sensing system operates at pH 9.0, exploiting the unique optical properties of BOCDs with emission peaks at 420 nm and 570 nm. In alkaline conditions, PHP develops a pink color that selectively quenches the 570 nm emission of the CDs while leaving the 420 nm peak unaffected. Upon introduction of GSH, the quinoid structure of PHP is converted to its benzenoid form via Michael addition, resulting in the disappearance of the pink color and subsequent restoration of the 570 nm fluorescence. This mechanism, utilized for the first time in GSH detection, offers a distinct advantage over previous methods that primarily relied on GSH's complexation capabilities. The analytical capabilities of the BOCDs/PHP ratiometric probe were extensively evaluated through multiple spectroscopic methods to understand its sensing mechanism. Performance analysis revealed impressive analytical figures of merit: the method exhibited strong linearity with a correlation coefficient of 0.9985, provided sensitive detection across a broad concentration range from 0.01 to 8.0 μM, and achieved a remarkably low detection limit of 3.33 nM. The method's versatility was enhanced through the development of a dual-mode smartphone platform, enabling both colorimetric and fluorometric GSH detection. Practical validation using human serum samples demonstrated the method's robustness in complex biological matrices, achieving high recovery rates between 98.5 % and 101.0 %, confirming its suitability for real-world clinical applications. This novel strategy combines the sensitivity of ratiometric fluorescence with the convenience of smartphone-based detection, offering a promising tool for GSH monitoring in clinical and research settings.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Glutathione
Ratiometric
Dual emission
Colorimetric
Smartphone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quantum yields measurement of BOCDs
Analysis of GSH in real samples
Morphological and structural characterization of BOCDs
Optimization of experimental parameters for gsh sensing
Optimization of the smartphone colorimetric detection of GSH
Determination of GSH via smartphone platform
Selectivity of BO-CDS/PHP based ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Possible mechanism for the detection of GSH
Detection of GSH in human serum samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image (Inset: size distribution histogram), (B) XRD pattern, (C) Energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX), and (D) FTIR spectra of BOCDs.
XPS spectrum of BOCDs. (A) Full scan spectrum, (B) C1 s spectrum, (C) N1 s spectrum and (D) O1 s spectrum.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence and excitation spectra of BOCDs (10 μg mL−1). (B) Photoluminescence emission spectra of BOCDs after exposing to varying excitation wavelengths ranging from 290 to 370 nm.
Effect of (A) the camera resolution, (B) aperture size, and (C) the ISO on the detection system.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of BOCDs (3.0 μg mL−1) in the GSH concentration range 0.01–8.0 μM. (B) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F570/F420) and the GSH concentration, where F570 and F420 represent the fluorescence intensity at 570 nm and 420 nm, respectively.

(A) A linear relationship between GSH concentration (5.0 – 15.0 μM) and red channel value under the irradiation of visible light (Inset: the images of the BOCDs with different concentrations of GSH under visible light). (B) A linear relationship between GSH concentration (2.0 – 12.0 μM) and b value under the UV irradiation (Inset: the images of the BOCDs with different concentrations of GSH under UV irradiation).
Response of the (BOCDs/PHP) system to different (A) amino acids, organic acids, biomolecules, (B) metal ions and anions.

(A) Emission spectrum overlay of BOCDs, BOCDs + PHP (pH 9.0), BOCDs + PHP+ GSH. (B) Overlapping of emission spectrum of BOCDs and UV visible absorption spectrum of PHP at pH 7.0 and pH 9.0 (C) Fluorescence lifetime of BOCDs in absence and in presence of PHP. (D) Spectral overlap of UV absorption spectra of PHP in presence of 0.0 μM, 1.0 μM, 2.0 μM and 3.0 μM GSH.
Results of the analysis of GSH in serum samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Quinoid to benzenoid transition-driven glutathione sensing: Dual-emission carbon dots and smartphone-based ratiometric fluorescence analysis

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Pharmacy, King Saud University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Pharmaceutics, College of Pharmacy, King Saud University, Riyadh, 11451, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Pharmacy, and Chemistry, Kingston University, Kingston-upon-Thames, London KT1 2EE, UK

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life Sciences, Pharmacy, and Chemistry, Kingston University, Kingston-upon-Thames

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cSchool of Life Sciences, Pharmacy, and Chemistry, Kingston University, Kingston-upon-Thames, London KT1 2EE, UK

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study presents a novel and sensitive method for the determination of glutathione (GSH), a crucial antioxidant and cellular protectant, using dual blue/orange-emitting carbon dots (BOCDs) and phenolphthalein (PHP). The sensing system operates at pH 9.0, exploiting the unique optical properties of BOCDs with emission peaks at 420 nm and 570 nm. In alkaline conditions, PHP develops a pink color that selectively quenches the 570 nm emission of the CDs while leaving the 420 nm peak unaffected. Upon introduction of GSH, the quinoid structure of PHP is converted to its benzenoid form via Michael addition, resulting in the disappearance of the pink color and subsequent restoration of the 570 nm fluorescence. This mechanism, utilized for the first time in GSH detection, offers a distinct advantage over previous methods that primarily relied on GSH's complexation capabilities. The analytical capabilities of the BOCDs/PHP ratiometric probe were extensively evaluated through multiple spectroscopic methods to understand its sensing mechanism. Performance analysis revealed impressive analytical figures of merit: the method exhibited strong linearity with a correlation coefficient of 0.9985, provided sensitive detection across a broad concentration range from 0.01 to 8.0 μM, and achieved a remarkably low detection limit of 3.33 nM. The method's versatility was enhanced through the development of a dual-mode smartphone platform, enabling both colorimetric and fluorometric GSH detection. Practical validation using human serum samples demonstrated the method's robustness in complex biological matrices, achieving high recovery rates between 98.5 % and 101.0 %, confirming its suitability for real-world clinical applications. This novel strategy combines the sensitivity of ratiometric fluorescence with the convenience of smartphone-based detection, offering a promising tool for GSH monitoring in clinical and research settings.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH) is a tripeptide made up of glutamate, glycine, and cysteine. It is the most prevalent non-protein thiol found inside cells and plays a vital role as an antioxidant, protecting living organisms from oxidative stress [1]. This crucial molecule is fundamental in preserving cellular redox balance, neutralizing harmful substances, modulating cell signaling pathways, and enhancing immune system function [2]. In physiological conditions, GSH concentrations vary widely across different matrices: serum levels typically range from 3.0 - 8.0 μM, while intracellular concentrations can reach up to 10.0 mM, particularly in hepatocytes [3]. Imbalanced GSH levels are linked to various health conditions. A decline in GSH is a marker of oxidative stress and has been associated with diseases such as cancer, neurodegenerative disorders, cardiovascular diseases, and liver dysfunction. Conversely, increased GSH levels may indicate an adaptive response to environmental toxins or the effects of certain medications [4]. Measuring GSH levels in various biological matrices is crucial for clinical diagnostics and toxicological assessments. In clinical settings, GSH levels act as a key biomarker for evaluating oxidative stress, liver function, and overall health status. In toxicology and environmental science, GSH analysis helps to evaluate the impact of pollutants and xenobiotics on biological systems [1]. The multifaceted significance of GSH across various fields underscores the critical need for accurate and reliable analytical methods for its determination in diverse matrices, driving ongoing research and development in analytical chemistry and biomedical sciences.

Previously reported techniques for GSH detection, including electrochemical [5,6], chromatographic [4,7], and colorimetric [8–12] methods, each present specific limitations. Electrochemical techniques are prone to interference from coexisting electroactive substances in biological environments, compromising selectivity, and may require electrode modification or pretreatment, which can be time-consuming and affect reproducibility. Chromatographic methods, particularly high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), while highly sensitive and specific, typically demand expensive equipment, specialized expertise, and lengthy sample preparation procedures, making them less suitable for rapid or routine analyses. Colorimetric assays, though simple and cost-effective, frequently lack the sensitivity required for sensing physiologically relevant GSH concentrations, especially in complex matrices. Many of these methods struggle to differentiate between reduced and oxidized forms of GSH without additional sample processing. Furthermore, many current methods are not easily adaptable to point-of-care or field-testing scenarios, limiting their utility in certain clinical or environmental applications.

Fluorometric methods using carbon dots for GSH detection offer several notable advantages [13–15]. Carbon dots are environmentally friendly, cost-effective, and easily synthesized nanomaterials with excellent photostability and low toxicity [16,17]. Their unique optical properties, including high quantum yield and tunable emission [18,19]. These nanomaterials exhibit good biocompatibility, making them suitable for both in vitro and potential in vivo applications [20,21]. The robust nature of carbon dots permits their use across a wide pH range and in various biological matrices.

Smartphone-based colorimetric detection has emerged as a powerful analytical tool that transforms everyday mobile devices into portable spectrophotometers. By leveraging the high-quality cameras and processing capabilities of modern smartphones, researchers have developed various applications for quantitative chemical analysis [22]. These systems typically combine specialized color-reactive test strips or solutions with smartphone image capture and analysis software to measure analyte concentrations. The built-in camera sensors can detect subtle color changes with remarkable sensitivity, while dedicated mobile apps employ sophisticated image processing algorithms to convert RGB values into quantitative measurements [23]. Recent advances have demonstrated successful applications in environmental monitoring, food safety testing, and point-of-care diagnostics, with some systems achieving detection limits comparable to laboratory-grade spectrophotometers. The ubiquity of smartphones, combined with their increasing computational power and camera quality, makes this approach particularly attractive for resource-limited settings where traditional analytical instruments may be unavailable [24].

The proposed method utilizing dual blue/orange-emitting carbon dots (BOCDs) and phenolphthalein (PHP) indicator at pH 9.0 for GSH detection offers several significant advantages over previously reported carbon dots based methods [25–28], particularly when combined with smartphone-based detection. Traditional approaches for GSH detection using carbon dots frequently encounter issues like environmental interference, complex sample preparation, and limited sensitivity. In contrast, the proposed dual-emission carbon dot method presents a novel and robust approach by leveraging the distinct properties of PHP at pH 9.0. Phenolphthalein, a pH-sensitive colorimetric indicator, turns pink in alkaline conditions, which effectively quenches one of the emission peaks of the BOCDs. The other emission peak remains unchanged and functions as a reference signal. This dual-emission system provides an intrinsic calibration mechanism, significantly enhancing the accuracy and reliability of the detection process. The orange and blue carbon dots serve distinct functions in this dual-emission detection system. The orange-emitting CDs act as the primary sensing element, with their fluorescence being initially quenched by the pink-colored PHP at pH 9.0. When GSH is introduced, it causes PHP to lose its color, leading to the restoration of the orange CDs' fluorescence intensity proportionally to GSH concentration. The blue-emitting CDs, on the other hand, function as an internal reference signal since their fluorescence remains stable and unaffected by both PHP and GSH throughout the detection process. This restoration is directly proportional to the concentration of GSH, allowing for a highly sensitive and selective ratiometric measurement. The use of a reference signal ensures that the detection is less susceptible to variations in environmental conditions, such as changes in pH, temperature, or the presence of other interfering substances. The integration of this method with smartphone-based detection further amplifies its advantages. This integration enables real-time monitoring and on-site analysis without the need for sophisticated laboratory equipment. The use of smartphone applications for data processing and analysis also adds a layer of convenience and accessibility, making it easier for users to conduct tests in various settings, including remote or resource-limited areas. Moreover, the dual-emission approach simplifies the detection process by eliminating the need for extensive sample preparation and complex instrumentation, which are often required in methods like HPLC and electrochemical techniques. This simplicity translates into a cost-effective and user-friendly assay, making it suitable for on-site and real-time monitoring of GSH levels in various matrices. The enhanced sensitivity and selectivity of the dual-emission carbon dot method are further underscored by its low limit of detection (LOD), which is comparable to or better than many existing methods. For instance, compared to single-emission carbon dot sensors that may have LODs in the micromolar range, the proposed method can achieve nanomolar sensitivity, thereby enabling the detection of even trace amounts of GSH.

The chemical reagents employed were of analytical grade. Essential amino acids including glycine, valine, proline, lysine, threonine, histidine, tryptophan, phenylalanine, leucine, and arginine were obtained from Fluka Analytical (New Jersey, USA). Additional biological compounds such as asparagine, serine, cysteine, homocysteine, glucose, ascorbic acid, and uric acid were also sourced from Fluka Analytical. Various metal salts and inorganic compounds were procured from El-Nasr Pharmaceutical Chemicals Co. (Cairo, Egypt), including chloride salts (sodium, potassium, magnesium, ferric), sulfate compounds (zinc, copper, sodium), and sodium-based chemicals (carbonate, bicarbonate, oxalate, citrate, disodium hydrogen phosphate). The key reagents phenolphthalein (PHP), glutathione (GSH), rhodamine 6 G, glacial acetic acid, ethylene diamine, boric acid, sodium hydroxide, and phosphoric acid were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany). Throughout the experiments, high-purity double distilled water (18.2 MΩ.cm) was utilized. The human serum samples were collected from volunteers at Assiut University Hospital (Assiut, Egypt).
Detailed information about the instrumentation used is provided in the Supplementary Data file.

The synthesis of BOCDs involved a hydrothermal process using rhodamine 6 G and ethylenediamine as precursors. Initially, a solution was prepared by dissolving 0.12 g of rhodamine 6 G and 30 μL of ethylenediamine in 25 mL of highly purified water. This mixture underwent brief sonication before being transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave for hydrothermal treatment. The reaction proceeded at 200 °C for 6 h. Upon completion and cooling to ambient temperature, the resulting BOCDs solution was purified through a two-step process. The purification protocol consisted of multiple sequential steps. Initial filtration through a 0.22 μm membrane removed suspended particles, followed by a 24-hour dialysis process using a 500 Da cut-off membrane to eliminate residual reactants and small molecular impurities. The purified BOCDs solution underwent lyophilization to yield the final solid product, which was stored at 4 °C until needed for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quantum yields measurement of BOCDs
ce:section.ce:para: A detailed explanation of the procedures and calculations can be found in the Supplementary Data file.

To determine GSH, first take 1.0 mL of BOCDs, 0.5 mL of PHP (500 μg mL−1), and 0.5 mL of Britton Robinson (BR) buffer (pH 9.0, 10 mM) into a 10 mL volumetric flask. Then, add 0.5 mL of varying concentrations of GSH to the flask and fill it to the mark with double distilled water, followed by incubation for 1 min. Prepare a blank solution following the same procedure but without the addition of GSH. Record the ratiometric fluorescence signal F570/F420 after excitation at 335 nm.

To adapt the GSH detection method for smartphone-based analysis, we followed a similar protocol with GSH concentrations ranging from 5.0 to 15.0 μM under visible light and from 2.0 to 12.0 μM under UV light. The resulting solutions were photographed under both visible light and UV illumination (365 nm) using a smartphone camera in a darkened environment. To standardize conditions, we used a white bulb and a UV lamp to simulate visible and UV light, respectively, and maintained a consistent 10 cm distance between the camera and the sample. We then extracted color values from these images using a color analysis application (Color analyzer app, version 2.0.2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of GSH in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate GSH detection in authentic human serum samples, we obtained blood specimens from the student health center at Assiut University hospital in Egypt. The samples underwent centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 5 mins, after which the supernatant was extracted and refrigerated for later use. We then diluted the serum 50 times with double distilled water. To create a range of spiked samples, we added various amounts of GSH to the diluted serum, resulting in concentrations from 0.01, 1.0, 2.0 μM. For quantification, we prepared a mixture in a 10 mL volumetric flask containing 1.0 mL of BOCDs, 0.5 mL of PHP (500 μg mL−1), and 0.5 mL of BR buffer (pH 9.0, 10 mM). We then introduced 0.5 mL of the GSH-containing serum samples to this mixture. Using our established method, we determined the GSH concentrations in these serum samples and subsequently calculated recovery values to assess the accuracy of our detection technique in complex biological matrices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characterization of BOCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and particle size of the BOCDs were determined using Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) imaging. The TEM images revealed that the particles are monodispersed, near spherical, and uniformly distributed, as shown in Fig. 1 A. The mean particle size ranges from 1.1 to 4.8 nm, with an average diameter of 2.8 nm, as indicated in the inset of Fig. 1A. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern reveals a broad peak positioned at 20.64° (Fig. 1B), which is characteristic of an amorphous structure. This broad, diffuse peak suggests a lack of long-range order in the material's atomic or molecular arrangement. Such a pattern is typically observed in materials with short-range order but no crystalline periodicity, indicating that the sample has an irregular, non-crystalline structure [29]. The elemental analysis of the BOCDs performed using the EDX method indicated 56.32, 28.41 and 7.89 % of C, O and N atoms, respectively (Fig. 1C). The FTIR spectrum of BOCDs exhibits several informative peaks (Fig. 1D). A broad band at 3208 cm⁻¹ suggests NH and OH stretching vibrations, while the peak at 2974 cm⁻¹ likely represents aliphatic CH stretching. The strong absorption at 1609 cm⁻¹ may be attributed to C = C or C = N stretching, with the 1500 cm⁻¹ peak potentially indicating NH bending or aromatic C = C stretching [30]. CN stretching might be responsible for the 1310 cm⁻¹ peak, whereas CO stretching could explain the 1171 cm⁻¹ signal. The band at 1018 cm⁻¹ may correspond to CO-C or CN stretching vibrations [31]. Finally, the peaks at 821 cm⁻¹ and 733 cm⁻¹ are likely due to out-of-plane CH bending in aromatic structures [32]. This spectral profile aligns with the expected functional groups and molecular features of carbon dots derived from the given precursors, highlighting the presence of amine, hydroxyl, aromatic, and various carbon-based moieties in the synthesized nanoparticles.

The XPS analysis reveals the detailed chemical composition and bonding environments within the sample. The survey scan shows three characteristic peaks at 286.32, 399.79, and 533.78 eV, corresponding to C1 s, N1 s, and O1 s core levels respectively (Fig. 2 A). High-resolution C1 s spectra deconvolution reveals three distinct carbon environments: CC/C = C bonds (284.25 eV), CN/CO single bonds (285.12 eV), and C = O groups (289.47 eV) (Fig. 2B). The N1 s region indicates three nitrogen groups: N = C (398.63 eV), NC (399.18 eV), and NH (401.08 eV) (Fig. 2C). The O1 s spectrum exhibits two oxygen environments: C = O (532.16 eV) and CO-C groups (533.32 eV) (Fig. 2D). This comprehensive XPS data suggests a carbon-based material featuring significant nitrogen doping and diverse oxygen-containing functional groups on its surface. [33].

The optical characteristics of the synthesized BOCDs were examined through UV–visible spectroscopy, revealing a complex absorption profile as shown in Fig. 3 A. Three distinct absorption bands were observed, each indicating specific electronic transitions within the material. A strong absorption at 230 nm suggests electronic transitions between π-π* orbitals in the aromatic carbon framework. Another prominent band appeared at 293 nm, which can be assigned to n-π* transitions originating from oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups. An additional absorption feature at 351 nm indicates the presence of surface-state transitions, likely arising from the various functional moieties decorating the carbon dot surface [34]. The peak at 521 nm is particularly interesting, as it confirms the presence of extended conjugated systems or specific chromophores, possibly inherited from the rhodamine 6 G precursor [35]. The fluorescence characteristics of these carbon dots are noteworthy, exhibiting two distinct emission peaks at 420 nm and 570 nm after excitation at 335 nm (Fig. 3A). The excitation-independent emission pattern observed when varying the excitation wavelength from 290 nm to 370 nm indicates that the emission originates from similar energy states, regardless of the excitation energy (Fig. 3B). This suggests a consistent electronic structure within the carbon dots. The choice of 335 nm as the optimal excitation wavelength maximizes the response of the 570 nm emission peak, which is crucial for the intended sensing application. This peak's sensitivity to both the added indicator and analyte makes it suitable for detection purposes, while the 420 nm peak serves as a stable reference signal. The initial high intensity of the 570 nm peak is strategically important, as it allows for a significant dynamic range when quenched by PHP addition. This design ensures that the sensing system remains responsive throughout the analysis. The quantum yield of 32 % indicates a relatively high fluorescence efficiency, which is beneficial for sensitive detection applications.

The sensing performance and reliability of the synthesized BOCDs were thoroughly investigated by monitoring their ratiometric fluorescence response (F570/F420) under different environmental challenges. These challenges included varying pH conditions, changes in ionic concentration, exposure to UV light for different durations, and temperature fluctuations. The pH study revealed that the highest fluorescence ratio was observed from pH 7.0 onwards, suggesting that acidic conditions may adversely affect the dual emission behavior of the carbon dots (Fig. S1A). This indicates that the carbon dots perform optimally in neutral to alkaline environments. Remarkably, the carbon dots demonstrated excellent stability across a wide range of ionic strengths (0.01 to 2.0 M) (Fig. S1B), with no significant changes in the fluorescence ratio. This ionic strength resistance is particularly advantageous for applications in complex biological or environmental samples. The carbon dots also exhibited impressive photostability, maintaining a nearly constant fluorescence ratio under UV irradiation for up to 180 mins (Fig. S1C). Temperature stability tests showed that the fluorescence ratio remained relatively constant from 20 °C to 40 °C, but began to change at temperatures above 40 °C. This thermal stability up to 40 °C suggests that these carbon dots could be suitable for applications involving moderate heating but may require careful consideration in high-temperature environments (Fig. S1D). These stability characteristics indicate that the prepared carbon dots are robust and well-suited for a wide range of sensing applications under various environmental conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of experimental parameters for gsh sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of GSH using the BOCDs and PHP sensing system is influenced by several critical experimental parameters. The system operates at pH 9.0, where the 570 nm fluorescence peak of BOCDs is initially quenched by PHP and subsequently restored upon GSH addition. The pH effect was investigated across a range of 6 to 12, with pH 9.0 emerging as optimal (Fig. S2A). At this pH, PHP turns pink, causing significant quenching of the 570 nm peak, which is crucial for the system's sensitivity. The concentration of PHP was another key factor, evaluated from 10 to 1000 μg mL−1. The most significant quenching and consequently the lowest fluorescence ratio was achieved at 500 μg mL−1 (Fig. S2B), which was selected for further experiments. Notably, the quenching effect of PHP on the carbon dots was immediate and stable, with no observable change in fluorescence intensity ratio over a period of 0 to 15 mins (Fig. S2C). Upon introduction of GSH to the BOCDs/ PHP system, the indicator begins to lose its color, leading to fluorescence restoration of the 570 nm peak. The kinetics of this interaction were studied by varying the reaction time between GSH and the sensing system from 0 to 15 mins (Fig. S2D). Interestingly, the fluorescence ratio reached its maximum after just 1.0 min, indicating rapid restoration of fluorescence. This 1.0-minute reaction time was chosen for subsequent studies, offering an optimal balance between speed and response magnitude.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the smartphone colorimetric detection of GSH
ce:section.ce:para: In order to enhance the detection capabilities of the colorimetric sensor integrated into the OPPO Reno7, the study meticulously investigated three primary smartphone camera parameters: resolution, aperture size, and ISO sensitivity, utilizing the " Color analyzer app " program to measure variations in color intensity. The research initially focused on camera resolution, recognizing that this parameter critically affects image clarity and detection performance. Despite the OPPO Reno7′s advanced 64 MP sensor, the experiments revealed that reducing the resolution to 12 MP provided superior colorimetric detection results, demonstrating that a moderate resolution can offer a more effective balance between capturing fine details and minimizing image noise (Fig. 4 A). Aperture size emerged as the second critical parameter, with the investigation exploring f-numbers ranging from f/0.95 to f/16 to understand its impact on light intake and color response. The OPPO Reno7 5G's fixed f/1.7 aperture was determined to be strategically optimal, representing an excellent compromise between light sensitivity and color contrast (Fig. 4B). This finding highlights the nuanced relationship between aperture size and optical performance, where wider apertures increase light capture, while narrower apertures enhance depth of field at the cost of reduced light intake. The final aspect of investigation centered on sensor sensitivity, examining the influence of ISO settings on photosensitive contrast color analysis. By systematically testing ISO ranges from 100 to 3200, the researchers observed a progressive increase in color intensity values, with ISO 800 emerging as the peak performance point (Fig. 4C). Beyond this value, the emergence of noise artifacts began to compromise the accuracy of color detection, underscoring the importance of identifying the precise sensitivity threshold that maximizes signal quality without introducing unwanted digital noise.

The BOCDs/PHP system's efficiency in detecting GSH was assessed under optimized conditions. As illustrated in Fig. 5 A, the dual-emission fluorescence of BOCDs responded to increasing GSH concentrations (0.01–8.0 μM) with a progressive enhancement of the 570 nm emission peak intensity, while the 420 nm peak remained stable. This differential response enables internal calibration, mitigating environmental interferences in GSH quantification. Fig. 5B depicts the correlation between the F570/F420 ratio and GSH concentration. A robust linear relationship was established within the 0.01–8.0 μM range, described by the equation F570/F420 = 0.27[CGSH] + 1.33, with a high correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9985). The method demonstrated high sensitivity, achieving a detection limit of 3.33 nM (S/N = 3). Comparative analysis with previously reported methods (Table S1) highlights the advantages of this approach, including its broad detection range and enhanced sensitivity. The dual-emission BOCDs sensor offers several benefits: it minimizes interference from various factors, is straightforward to operate, cost-effective, and provides rapid results. These characteristics make it particularly suitable for efficient GSH determination in practical applications. The unique ratiometric fluorescence response of this system, coupled with its simplicity and speed, positions it as a promising tool for GSH analysis in complex environments where traditional methods may fall short.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of GSH via smartphone platform
ce:section.ce:para: To enable more convenient on-site analysis, a smartphone-based platform was developed for the monitoring of GSH using the BOCDs/PHP sensing system. Interestingly, the reaction between BOCDs/PHP and different GSH concentrations induced significant color changes under both visible and ultraviolet light conditions. Leveraging this visual response, a smartphone-based imaging colorimetry approach was established for rapid, in-field detection of GSH. Compared to naked-eye visual assessment, the smartphone imaging colorimetry technique offers distinct advantages in precisely identifying and quantifying the color changes. The process involves capturing a series of visible and fluorescent images of the detection system using the smartphone camera (Fig. 6 A and 6B). These images are then converted into color parameters values using a mobile application (Color analyzer). When varying concentrations of GSH (5.0 – 15.0 μM) were added to the BOCDs/PHP solution, the characteristic pink color of the solution gradually faded under visible light (Fig. 6A). Analysis of the captured visible light images revealed a strong linear correlation between the red color parameter and GSH concentration, described by the equation R = – 4.64[GSH] + 212.27 (R2 = 0.9917). This inverse relationship between the red value and GSH concentration allowed for a LOD of 1.38 μM using the visible light-based colorimetric approach. Under UV irradiation, the fluorescence of the BOCDs/PHP system was observed to change progressively with increasing GSH concentrations (2.0 – 12.0 μM) (Fig. 6B). The b value from the fluorescence images exhibited a robust linear correlation with GSH concentration, expressed by the equation b = 3.50[GSH] – 59.38 (R2 = 0.9966). The b parameter indicates the position along the blue-yellow color axis, where negative b values reflect blue tones and positive b values correspond to yellow tones in the color space. As the concentration of GSH increases, the orange emission is gradually restored, causing the b value to shift positively, indicating a movement toward the yellow end of the spectrum. This linear relationship enabled an LOD of 0.82 μM for GSH detection using the smartphone-based fluorescence imaging approach. The insets of Fig. 6A and 6B also display the parameters derived from the values obtained using the color analyzer. R and b values were selected as signals due to their strong correlation with GSH concentration.

The smartphone-based colorimetric and fluorescence imaging methods leverage the visual and spectral changes induced by GSH in the BOCDs/PHP system, providing simple, on-site detection capabilities. The high linearity and sensitivity of these smartphone-based quantification techniques underscore their potential for practical applications in GSH monitoring, complementing the laboratory-based fluorescence measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of BO-CDS/PHP based ratiometric fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the practical applicability of the developed sensor, we evaluated its selectivity and resistance to interference, crucial factors given the complexity of biological samples. We examined the impact of various potential interferents on the BOCDs' fluorescence intensity, including a range of metal ions (Mg2+, Na+, K+, Fe3+, Cu2+, and Zn2+), anions (chloride, carbonate, sulphate, phosphate, oxalate, and citrate), amino acids (including phenylalanine, cysteine, tryptophan, leucine, proline, asparagine, valine, serine, histidine, lysine, threonine, homocysteine, glycine, arginine), and other biologically relevant molecules (glucose, uric acid, ascorbic acid). Cyanide ion (CN−) was specifically used as a masking agent for Fe³⁺ and Cu²⁺ to prevent interference. Additionally, to minimize interference from ascorbic acid, ascorbate oxidase can be utilized to catalyze its oxidation into dehydroascorbic acid, effectively preventing unwanted interactions with the benzoquinone moiety of PHP. Fig. 7 A and Fig. 7B illustrate the changes in F570/F420 ratio upon addition of 4.0 μM GSH compared to 50.0 μM of each potential interferent. The results clearly demonstrate that only GSH induces a significant change in the F570/F420 ratio, while other substances have negligible effects, highlighting the sensor's excellent selectivity towards GSH. These findings collectively indicate that the BOCDs-based sensor possesses remarkable selectivity and resistance to interference, making it a promising ratiometric fluorescent tool for GSH detection in complex biological matrices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism for the detection of GSH
ce:section.ce:para: The BOCDs/PHP sensing system for GSH detection operates at an optimized pH of 9.0. At this pH, the introduction of PHP leads to a significant quenching of the 570 nm emission peak of the dual emission BOCDs. This quenching effect is attributed to the development of a pink color in the solution due to the alkaline pH, which effectively suppresses the 570 nm fluorescence. Importantly, the 420 nm emission peak remains relatively constant during this process, serving as a stable reference signal. Upon the addition of GSH to the BOCDs/PHP system, a remarkable change occurs. The pink color of the solution starts to fade, indicating a reaction between GSH and PHP. Concomitantly, the fluorescence intensity of the 570 nm peak begins to be restored, while the 420 nm peak maintains its intensity (Fig. 8 A). This differential response of the dual-emission BOCDs allows for ratiometric analysis.

To explore the underlying interaction process, we characterized the changes in the BOCDs upon the addition of PHP using UV–Vis absorption/fluorescence spectroscopy and fluorescence lifetime measurements. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of PHP reveals that its main absorption peak overlaps with the 570 nm emission peak of the BOCDs, but not with the 420 nm emission peak (Fig. 8B). Although there is a small peak at 355 nm of PHP, there is no change in fluorescence at 420 nm, likely due to the weak absorption value at this wavelength. This selective overlap suggests that the 570 nm emission is more susceptible to quenching, while the 420 nm emission remains relatively unaffected. This observation points to the potential involvement of either an inner filter effect (IFE) or Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) in the fluorescence quenching process. To further elucidate the precise quenching mechanism, we investigated the fluorescence lifetime of the BOCDs before and after the addition of GSH. As shown in Fig. 8C, the fluorescence lifetime of the BOCDs changed from 7.92 ns to 10.23 ns upon GSH introduction. This shortening of the fluorescence lifetime indicates that FRET is the dominant quenching mechanism in this system. FRET is a distance-dependent energy transfer mechanism that takes place when the emission spectrum of the donor (BOCDs) overlaps with the absorption spectrum of the acceptor (PHP), resulting in the quenching of the 570 nm emission.

The disappearance of the pink color of PHP upon the addition of GSH can be explained by the transformation of the quinoid structure of PHP to its hydroquinone form. This transformation is based on the well-documented interaction between GSH and benzoquinone species [36–38]. In its alkaline, deprotonated form at pH 9.0, PHP exists in a quinoid structure, which imparts the characteristic pink color to the solution. However, in the presence of GSH, PHP spontaneously reacted with GSH to form GS-hydroquinones via Michael addition (Fig. S3). This transformation results in the fading of the pink color, as the hydroquinone structure does not have a conjugated structure. By leveraging this well-established principle, the developed BOCDs/PHP sensing system exploits the GSH-induced color change of PHP as a means to detect and quantify GSH levels. The disappearance of the pink color, which is associated with the 570 nm fluorescence quenching of the BOCDs, serves as a visual and fluorometric indicator of the presence and concentration of GSH in the sample. This dual-modal detection approach, combining the color change and ratiometric fluorescence response, enhances the reliability and sensitivity of the GSH sensing platform. The interaction between GSH and PHP is further evidenced by the changes observed in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of PHP, as illustrated in Fig. 8D. Upon the addition of GSH, the distinct absorption peak of PHP at 555 nm is observed to weaken significantly. Concurrently, the color of the PHP solution fades gradually, as shown in the inset of Fig. 6A.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical application of the optimized BOCDs/PHP ratiometric fluorescence method was assessed through analysis of human serum samples. The method's high sensitivity enabled significant dilution of serum samples, effectively reducing potential interference from co-existing substances. Serum aliquots were spiked with exogenous GSH at physiologically relevant concentrations (0.01, 1.0, and 2.0 μM). The results presented in Table 1 demonstrate excellent recovery rates ranging from 98.5 % to 101.0 %, with relative standard deviations not exceeding 2.77 %. Notably, the accuracy and precision achieved with serum samples were comparable to those obtained using standard solutions, indicating the sensor's effectiveness in managing matrix effects from various biological components. The successful quantification of GSH in these complex physiological samples highlights the method's potential for clinical applications. Method validation included comparison with both a traditional HPLC technique using o-phthalaldehyde pre-column derivatization and an established fluorometric method for GSH determination [39]. The proposed method was also compared with a colorimetric method [40], showing no significant difference in percentage recovery. The absence of significant differences in percentage recovery values between the methods supports the validity of the proposed method.

In conclusion, this study presents a novel and highly sensitive ratiometric fluorescence method for glutathione (GSH) detection, utilizing dual-emission carbon dots and phenolphthalein as an indicator. The unique approach, based on the pH-dependent color change of phenolphthalein and its interaction with GSH, offers significant advantages over traditional complexation-based methods. The developed sensing system demonstrates excellent analytical performance, including high sensitivity, wide linear range, and low detection limit, while also showing remarkable selectivity towards GSH in complex biological matrices. The successful adaptation to a smartphone-based platform for both colorimetric and fluorometric detection enhances the method's accessibility and potential for point-of-care applications. Furthermore, the comprehensive characterization of the sensing mechanism and its successful application in human serum samples underscore the robustness and practical utility of this approach. The developed approach represents a significant advancement in GSH detection methodology while establishing a foundation for designing new ratiometric fluorescence sensors targeting other vital biological analytes, with potential applications ranging from clinical diagnostics to environmental monitoring.

Al-Montaser Bellah H. Ali: Visualization, Validation, Software, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Ashraf M. Mahmoud: Visualization, Validation, Resources, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Yousef A. Bin Jardan: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Resources, Validation, Writing – original draft. Aya M. Mostafa: Conceptualization, Data curation, Methodology, Project administration, Software, Validation, Visualization. James Barker: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Mohamed M. El-Wekil: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to extend their sincere appreciation to the Researchers Supporting Project, King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia for funding this work through the project number (RSP2025R457).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.talo.2025.100418.
